header.hero: The most painful condition known to medicine.
header.subtitle: Live clinical trials, research papers, and treatment information for cluster headache — updated on every page load.

overview.title: What Is Cluster Headache?
overview.subtitle: Key facts, epidemiology, and the current research landscape

basics.title: The Basics
basics.pain: Excruciating unilateral orbital, supraorbital, or temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes — often called "suicide headache"
basics.autonomic: Tearing, red eye, drooping eyelid, nasal congestion, sweating — all on the pain side
basics.pattern: 1–8 attacks/day, frequently occurring during sleep, clustering in periods of weeks to months
basics.types: Episodic (cycles with remission) vs. Chronic (no remission >3 months)
basics.prevalence: ~0.1% of population; 3–4× more common in men historically, though the gender gap is narrowing

research.title: Current Research Focus Areas
research.psychedelics: Multiple Phase 2 LSD RCTs actively recruiting; psilocybin trials publishing results
research.cgrp: Galcanezumab approved for episodic CH; others showed mixed results
research.sleep: SUNCET trial testing sodium oxybate for nocturnal attacks in chronic CH
research.genetics: Multiple risk loci identified in recent GWAS studies (Harder et al., Dahl et al.)
research.biomarkers: MicroRNA profiling underway at IRCCS Mondino, Italy

approved.title: What's Currently Approved
approved.acute: High-flow O₂ (most effective), SC sumatriptan 6mg, intranasal zolmitriptan 5mg
approved.transitional: Greater occipital nerve block, short prednisone course
approved.device: gammaCore (FDA-cleared nVNS) for episodic acute & chronic prevention

disclaimer.title: Medical Disclaimer
disclaimer.text: This site is for informational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. All clinical trial information is sourced directly from ClinicalTrials.gov. Some substances discussed (psilocybin, LSD, LSA) are controlled or illegal in many jurisdictions — this site does not advocate their use. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional before changing your treatment.

gap.title: The Treatment Gap — What New Patients Need to Know
gap.subtitle: Most CH patients spend years receiving ineffective or counterproductive treatment before finding what actually works.
gap.delay: CH is frequently misdiagnosed as migraine, sinusitis, or dental pain. Many patients see 5+ doctors before getting the correct diagnosis.
gap.oxygen: High-flow O₂ is the safest, fastest, most effective treatment with zero side effects. Yet many patients go years without being offered it. If your doctor hasn't mentioned oxygen, ask — or find a headache specialist who will.
gap.failures: Oral triptans (too slow — only SC injection works for CH), standard migraine preventives (topiramate, amitriptyline — ineffective for CH), opioids (don't work, create dependency), and verapamil dosed too low (<360mg is usually insufficient).
gap.worse: SSRIs and SNRIs can increase attack frequency for some patients and block psychedelic treatments. Beta-blockers are ineffective. Overuse of triptans can cause rebound.
gap.community: Treatments like the Vitamin D3 regimen (80% responder rate in surveys), busting protocols (now in Phase 2 trials), and high-flow oxygen technique refinements all came from patients sharing what works — often years before clinical research caught up.

trials.subtitle: All cluster headache trials currently recruiting, active, or starting soon — fetched live from ClinicalTrials.gov

treatments.title: Clinical Treatments
treatments.subtitle: Evidence-based treatments — approved drugs, devices, and procedural options
treatments.newPatientWarning: Many GPs and neurologists are not CH specialists and may prescribe treatments that are ineffective or counterproductive for cluster headache (oral triptans, standard migraine preventives, opioids). If your current treatment isn't working, seek a headache specialist and explore the Community tab for patient-tested approaches.
treatments.prescribingFailuresDesc: These are frequently prescribed to CH patients by non-specialist doctors but are ineffective or harmful. If you're receiving any of these as your primary CH treatment, seek a headache specialist.
treatments.comparisonTitle: What Actually Works — Community Consensus vs Standard Care
treatments.comparisonDesc: Based on decades of patient experience from Clusterbusters, r/ClusterHeadaches, and patient surveys. Many of these are now validated by clinical research.

o2.name: High-flow O₂ (15–25 L/min)
o2.community: The #1 treatment. Get this first. 78% efficacy, zero side effects. Demand-valve mask is fastest.

d3.name: Vitamin D3 Batch Protocol
d3.community: 80% responder rate in surveys. Start at 10,000 IU/day with cofactors (K2, Mg, Omega-3). Begin early in cycle.

sumatriptan.name: SC sumatriptan 6mg
sumatriptan.community: Best pharma abortive when O₂ unavailable. Injection only — oral is useless for CH. Carry at all times.

energy.name: Energy drink at shadow onset
energy.community: Caffeine + taurine at first sign of an attack. Free, instant, no side effects. Works for many.

melatonin.name: Melatonin 10–25mg
melatonin.community: RCT-backed at 10mg. Much higher than sleep dosing. Take before usual attack time. OTC, safe.

verapamil.name: Verapamil (360–960mg)
verapamil.community: Standard preventive but must be dosed high enough. Most GPs start too low. Push for headache specialist dosing.

gon.name: GON block (bridge)
gon.community: Fast-acting bridge while verapamil takes effect. Can break an active cycle. Ask neurologist.

busting.name: Busting (psilocybin/LSA)
busting.community: Life-changing for many refractory patients. Now in Phase 2 RCTs. See Community tab for protocol.
busting.doctor: Ignored by doctors — life-changing for many

o2.title: High-Flow Oxygen
items: 12–25 L/min via non-rebreather (NRB) mask
78% efficacy in pivotal RCT (Cohen 2009, BMJ) at 12 L/min; higher flows often more effective
15–25 min duration or until resolved
Hyperventilation technique speeds abort
Demand-valve mask: fastest community method

sumatriptan.title: Sumatriptan SC 6mg
items: Onset ~10 minutes; most reliable pharma abortive
Subcutaneous injection only (oral too slow)
Intranasal zolmitriptan 5mg as alternative
Triptans block serotonergic treatments (see Community tab for details)
Limit use to avoid medication overuse

gammacore.title: gammaCore (nVNS)
items: Non-invasive vagus nerve stimulator (neck device)
FDA-cleared: acute episodic + prevention chronic CH
ACT1/ACT2 trials: superior to sham for episodic CH
PREVA trial: superior to standard care alone (chronic)
Can be used at home, no prescription for device

civamide.title: Civamide (Zucapsaicin)
items: Synthetic capsaicin analogue, intranasal
Phase 3 trials showed efficacy for episodic CH
Development stalled; not commercially available
Depletes substance P from trigeminal fibers

verapamil.title: Verapamil
items: Most widely used preventive; onset 2–3 weeks
Effective dose: 360–960 mg/day (divided doses)
ECG monitoring required at higher doses (AV block risk)
Active cardiac safety trial: NCT04406259 (Nice, FR)
Bridge with GON block or prednisone while starting

galcanezumab.title: Galcanezumab (Emgality)
items: Only anti-CGRP mAb approved for CH (episodic only)
Monthly 300mg injection; onset within days
Did not meet primary endpoints in chronic CH trials
Fremanezumab & erenumab: negative in CH trials
Eptinezumab: mixed results, some benefit at week 4

lithium.title: Lithium
items: Effective for chronic CH, especially refractory
Narrow therapeutic window (0.6–1.2 mEq/L)
Regular blood monitoring required
Consider with verapamil under specialist supervision

melatonin.title: Melatonin (10–25mg)
items: RCT: 50% responder rate at 10mg at bedtime (n=20)
Particularly effective for nocturnal attacks
Excellent tolerability; OTC in most countries
10mg is the RCT-studied dose; doses above this are empirical

gonBlock.title: GON Block
items: Corticosteroid + local anesthetic at skull base
Onset 24–72h; duration 2–8 weeks
Use as bridge while verapamil takes effect
Phase 3 trial ongoing: NCT05324748 (Leiden)
Also: repeated GON blocks being studied as prevention

spg.title: SPG Stimulation
items: Implantable sphenopalatine ganglion neurostimulator
On-demand acute relief; strong evidence base
For refractory cases when medications fail
Pulsed radiofrequency (PRF) non-implant alternative in trials (NCT06787677)

psilocybin.title: Psilocybin
items: Repeat pulse reduces attacks by 50% (2024 RCT)
Australian MRFF-funded trial underway (2025)
Health Canada: first-ever legal access for CH (2024)
Sub-hallucinogenic doses appear sufficient

lsd.title: LSD (Micro/Low Dose)
items: Basel RCT: 3×100µg, double-blind crossover (NCT03781128)
Radboud RCT: minidosing for chronic CH (NCT05477459)
Both recruiting; results expected 2025–2027
Community use ("busting") for 20+ years

ketamine.title: Ketamine + Magnesium IV
items: Single IV infusion for refractory chronic CH
Pilot: 13/17 patients ≥50% attack reduction
14 sites in France; 90 patients (NCT04814381)
Primary completion Sep 2026
ketamine.note: Phase 4 designation reflects ketamine's approved status for other indications (anesthesia), not for CH.

oxybate.title: Sodium Oxybate (SUNCET)
items: Targets nocturnal CH via sleep deepening (↑SWS, ↓REM)
RCT at Leiden University (NCT06950281)
n=52; starts Jan 2026; results expected Dec 2027
Wearable sleep monitoring included

light.title: Light Therapy
items: Luminettes® device for chronic CH prevention
Hypothesis: circadian/hypothalamic reset
n=48, Marseille (NCT06540651); ends Feb 2027

botox.title: Botulinum Toxin A (SPG)
items: SPG blockade with BoNT-A for refractory chronic CH
5 European sites; 112 patients (NCT03944876)
Norwegian University of Science & Technology
Primary completion Sep 2025

oralTriptans.title: Oral Triptans
items: Oral sumatriptan/rizatriptan onset is 30–60 min — far too slow for CH attacks lasting 15–180 min
Only subcutaneous injection (onset ~10 min) or nasal spray works for CH
Commonly prescribed by GPs unfamiliar with CH — ask for SC sumatriptan

opioids.title: Opioids / Painkillers
items: Opioids do not abort CH attacks — the attack typically ends before they take effect
Create dependency with no therapeutic benefit for CH
Often prescribed in emergency rooms by doctors unfamiliar with the condition

migrainePreventives.title: Standard Migraine Preventives
items: Topiramate, amitriptyline, propranolol — effective for migraine but not for CH
Frequently prescribed due to misdiagnosis as migraine
Months wasted on ineffective treatment while attacks continue

ssris.title: SSRIs / SNRIs
items: May increase attack frequency in some CH patients
Block serotonin receptors needed for psychedelic treatments (busting)
Require 2–5 week washout before busting can be attempted
Discuss alternatives with your doctor if considering other treatment paths

community.title: Patient Community Treatments
community.subtitle: Empirical knowledge from Clusterbusters, r/ClusterHeadaches, and CH patient groups — not medical advice
community.whyMatters: Many of the most effective CH treatments were discovered by patients, not doctors. High-flow oxygen technique refinements, the Vitamin D3 regimen, energy drinks at shadow onset, and the psychedelic busting protocol — all came from patients sharing what works. Several are now being validated in clinical trials, but the community was using them effectively for years before research caught up. If your doctor's treatment plan isn't working, the knowledge here may change your life.
community.warning: These treatments are reported by patients and are not all officially approved. Always discuss with your doctor — but know that many CH patients find their best treatment through community knowledge, not their initial prescription.
community.bustingDesc: "Busting" is the patient-community term for using psychedelic substances to break CH cycles. Popularized and formally organized by Bob Wold, who founded Clusterbusters after his own experience. Now being evaluated in Phase 2 clinical trials.
community.vitaminDDesc: Developed by Pete Batcheller (CH sufferer, retired Navy pilot). Survey of 110 patients: 80% reported significant reduction in frequency, duration, and severity. Formal clinical trial NCT04570475 ongoing.

1.name: High-flow O₂ (12–25 L/min, NRB mask)
1.why: Fastest, safest, most effective abortive. Get it prescribed first. Clusterbusters has free doctor letter templates.

2.name: SC sumatriptan 6mg
2.why: Best pharmaceutical abortive when O₂ unavailable. Onset ~10 min. Keep on you at all times.

3.name: Energy drink at shadow onset
3.why: Caffeine + taurine at the very first sign. Free, instant, works for some. Nothing to lose by trying.

4.name: Melatonin 10–25mg at bedtime
4.why: Safe OTC, RCT-backed for nocturnal attacks. Take well before usual attack time.

5.name: Verapamil (push for 360–960mg)
5.why: Standard first-line preventive. Most GPs dose too low. Ask for a referral to a headache specialist.

6.name: Vitamin D3 Batch Protocol
6.why: 80% of survey respondents report significant improvement. Start early in cycle. See vitamindregimen.com.

7.name: GON block (bridge therapy)
7.why: Ask neurologist. Fast-acting bridge while verapamil takes effect. Can break an active cycle.

8.name: Busting (psilocybin/LSA seeds)
8.why: Last resort for many; transforms life for some. Read Clusterbusters forums thoroughly before attempting. Now in Phase 2 RCTs.

bustingSubstances: LSA seeds: Rivea corymbosa (50–100 seeds) or Hawaiian Baby Woodrose (4–8 seeds). Potency varies greatly by batch — consult Clusterbusters forums for current dosing guidance
bustingProtocol: Typical course: 3 doses; many report cycle breaking after 1–2
Stop triptans 5+ days before. SSRIs/SNRIs require much longer washout (2–5 weeks depending on drug) — consult your doctor before stopping any antidepressant
d3Core: Some titrate to 20,000–50,000 IU based on blood tests

triggers.title: Triggers & Avoidance
triggers.subtitle: Community-identified triggers — especially relevant during active cluster cycles

alcohol.name: Alcohol (any type)
alcohol.notes: Even a small amount reliably triggers attacks during an active bout. The community universally avoids all alcohol during cycles.

altitude.name: Altitude / Low oxygen
altitude.notes: Flights, mountains, high altitude environments. Some patients pre-treat with supplemental O₂ before flying.

smells.name: Strong chemical smells
smells.notes: Gasoline, paint fumes, perfume, cleaning products, solvents. Wear a mask if exposure is unavoidable.

sleep.name: Sleep disruption
sleep.notes: CH attacks frequently occur during or around REM sleep transitions. Jet lag, shift work, naps, or any disruption to sleep schedule.

vasodilating.name: Vasodilating drugs
vasodilating.notes: Nitrates (GTN sprays), sildenafil/tadalafil, some blood pressure medications. Always inform prescribers about CH.

histamine.name: Histamine-rich foods
histamine.notes: Aged cheeses, cured meats, fermented foods. Red wine is a double trigger — both histamine and alcohol/vasodilation.

heat.name: Heat exposure
heat.notes: Hot baths, saunas, intense exercise in heat. Individual variation — some find hot showers offer temporary relief during an attack.

light.name: Bright / flickering light
light.notes: Less consistent than migraine triggers but reported by a subset of patients, especially during attacks.

season.name: Season changes
season.notes: Many episodic CH patients have predictable seasonal patterns (spring, autumn). Useful to anticipate and start preventives early.

stress.name: Stress let-down
stress.notes: Attacks often occur after stress resolves — weekends, vacations, holidays. Classic pattern in episodic patients.

triggers.highRiskDesc: Reliably trigger attacks during active cycles
triggers.mediumRiskDesc: Reported by many but not universal
triggers.lowRiskDesc: Inconsistent or predictive — useful to be aware of
activeCycleItems: Zero alcohol — even one drink can trigger an attack within 30–60 minutes
Maintain strict sleep schedule; protect sleep timing
Carry O₂ or sumatriptan injection at all times
Inform airline staff / travel with O₂ documentation
Avoid scented products and chemical environments
Pre-treat with O₂ before known trigger exposure where possible
Keep a headache diary to identify personal triggers
Alert family/colleagues — attacks are very visible and frightening

footer.communityInfo: Community info from Clusterbusters, r/ClusterHeadaches, and patient survey literature.

common.oxygenIsNumber1: Oxygen is the #1 abortive — but most doctors don't prescribe it.
common.medicationsMakeWorse: Medications that can make things worse:
